name: Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive
description: EU Directive 2022/2464 requiring companies to report on sustainability matters using European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS). Replaces and expands the Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD).
applicableTo: Companies meeting 2 of 3: >250 employees, >EUR 50M revenue, >EUR 25M total assets

requirements.title: Sustainability reporting in management report
requirements.description: Companies must include sustainability information in the management report, covering environmental, social, and governance matters.

requirements.title: Double materiality assessment
requirements.description: Companies must assess both impact materiality (company's impact on people and environment) and financial materiality (sustainability matters affecting the company's financials).

requirements.title: Business model and strategy resilience
requirements.description: Describe the resilience of the business model and strategy to sustainability risks, including climate transition plans.

requirements.title: Assurance of sustainability reporting
requirements.description: Sustainability reports must be subject to limited assurance (moving to reasonable assurance by 2028).

requirements.title: Digital tagging (XBRL)
requirements.description: Sustainability information must be marked up in XHTML using ESEF digital taxonomy.

penalties: Member States set penalties. Fines for non-compliance vary by country. Directors can face personal liability.

timeline.FY2024: Large listed companies (former NFRD scope) - first reports due 2025
timeline.FY2025: Large companies meeting 2 of 3 thresholds - first reports due 2026
timeline.FY2026: Listed SMEs (may delay until FY2028) - first reports due 2027
timeline.FY2028: Non-EU companies with >EUR 150M EU revenue - first reports due 2029

data.assuranceProvider: Statutory auditor or independent assurance provider

name: European Sustainability Reporting Standards - Cross-Cutting Standards
description: ESRS 1 (General Requirements) and ESRS 2 (General Disclosures) are the foundational cross-cutting standards applicable to all CSRD reporters. ESRS 2 is always mandatory.

requirements.title: General Requirements
requirements.description: Sets out the architecture, conventions, and fundamental concepts for ESRS reporting including double materiality, value chain, time horizons, and due diligence.

requirements.title: Role of administrative, management and supervisory bodies
requirements.description: Disclose composition, roles, and ESG expertise of governance bodies.

requirements.title: Information provided to and sustainability matters addressed by governance bodies
requirements.description: Disclose how governance bodies are informed and address sustainability topics.

requirements.title: Integration of sustainability into incentive schemes
requirements.description: Disclose whether and how sustainability performance is linked to remuneration.

requirements.title: Strategy, business model and value chain
requirements.description: Describe business model, strategy, and value chain in relation to sustainability.

requirements.title: Material impacts, risks and opportunities
requirements.description: Describe material impacts, risks, and opportunities arising from the double materiality assessment.

requirements.title: Process to identify and assess material IROs
requirements.description: Describe the process used to identify and assess impacts, risks, and opportunities.

data.note: ESRS 2 is the only fully mandatory standard; all other topical standards are subject to materiality assessment

name: European Sustainability Reporting Standards - Environmental Standards
description: ESRS Environmental standards covering climate change (E1), pollution (E2), water and marine resources (E3), biodiversity and ecosystems (E4), and resource use and circular economy (E5).
applicableTo: Companies subject to CSRD where topic is material

requirements.title: Climate Change
requirements.description: Transition plans, GHG emission targets (Scope 1/2/3), energy consumption, carbon pricing exposure, financial effects of climate risks. Includes alignment with 1.5C pathway requirements.

requirements.title: Gross Scopes 1, 2, 3 and Total GHG emissions
requirements.description: Mandatory GHG disclosure using GHG Protocol methodology. Scope 3 can be phased in over first year.

requirements.title: Anticipated financial effects from climate-related physical and transition risks
requirements.description: Quantified financial effects of physical and transition risks on assets, revenue, and costs.

requirements.title: Pollution
requirements.description: Pollution of air, water, soil; substances of concern; microplastics. Includes pollutant emission targets.

requirements.title: Water and Marine Resources
requirements.description: Water consumption, withdrawal, discharge; water stress areas; marine resource management.

requirements.title: Biodiversity and Ecosystems
requirements.description: Impact on biodiversity; land use change; species at risk; ecosystem services; alignment with Kunming-Montreal GBF.

requirements.title: Resource Use and Circular Economy
requirements.description: Resource inflows/outflows; waste management; product design for circularity; recycled content.

data.E1_datapoints: ~80 datapoints including transition plan, targets, GHG emissions, energy, carbon credits
data.E2_datapoints: ~30 datapoints on pollutant emissions and substances of concern
data.E3_datapoints: ~25 datapoints on water consumption, stress areas
data.E4_datapoints: ~40 datapoints on biodiversity impact and dependencies
data.E5_datapoints: ~30 datapoints on resource use and waste
data.note: Subject to materiality assessment except ESRS E1 transition plan disclosure which has heightened expectations

name: European Sustainability Reporting Standards - Social and Governance Standards
description: ESRS Social standards covering own workforce (S1), workers in the value chain (S2), affected communities (S3), consumers and end-users (S4), plus Governance standard on business conduct (G1).
applicableTo: Companies subject to CSRD where topic is material

requirements.title: Own Workforce
requirements.description: Working conditions, equal treatment, health & safety, training, living wages, social dialogue, workforce diversity metrics, pay gap reporting.

requirements.title: Workers in the Value Chain
requirements.description: Due diligence on working conditions, forced labor, child labor in supply chain. Remediation processes.

requirements.title: Affected Communities
requirements.description: Impact on local and indigenous communities, land rights, health impacts, community engagement.

requirements.title: Consumers and End-Users
requirements.description: Product safety, data privacy, accessibility, responsible marketing, financial inclusion.

requirements.title: Business Conduct
requirements.description: Anti-corruption, anti-bribery, political engagement, lobbying, payment practices, supplier relationships, whistleblower protection.

data.S1_datapoints: ~90 datapoints including diversity, pay gap, H&S, training
data.S2_datapoints: ~30 datapoints on supply chain labor practices

name: EU Taxonomy for Sustainable Activities
description: Regulation (EU) 2020/852 establishing a classification system for environmentally sustainable economic activities. Requires disclosure of revenue, CapEx, and OpEx alignment.

requirements.title: Criteria for environmentally sustainable activities
requirements.description: An activity is taxonomy-aligned if it: (1) substantially contributes to one of 6 objectives, (2) does no significant harm (DNSH) to the other 5, (3) complies with minimum safeguards (OECD Guidelines, UNGPs).

requirements.title: Transparency of undertakings in non-financial statements
requirements.description: Non-financial companies must disclose proportion of Taxonomy-eligible and Taxonomy-aligned turnover, CapEx, and OpEx.

requirements.title: Financial product disclosures
requirements.description: Financial products marketed as sustainable must disclose the proportion of Taxonomy-aligned investments.

sixObjectives.name: Sustainable use and protection of water and marine resources
Protection and restoration of biodiversity and ecosystems

climateChange_mitigation_examples.buildings_newConstruction: Primary energy demand at least 10% below nZEB threshold
climateChange_mitigation_examples.manufacturing_steel: GHG emissions below sector benchmarks (EU ETS benchmarks)

minimumSafeguards: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights

controversialInclusions.naturalGas: Included as transitional activity under strict conditions (lifecycle <270 gCO2e/kWh, switching from coal)
controversialInclusions.nuclear: Included as transitional activity (new plants with approved waste management plan)

name: Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation
description: Regulation (EU) 2019/2088 requiring financial market participants to disclose sustainability-related information at entity and product level. Classifies products as Article 6, 8, or 9.

requirements.title: Entity-level sustainability risk integration
requirements.description: Publish policies on integration of sustainability risks in investment decision-making.

requirements.title: Principal Adverse Impact (PAI) statements
requirements.description: Entity-level disclosure of principal adverse impacts of investment decisions on sustainability factors. Comply-or-explain for entities <500 employees.

requirements.title: Product-level sustainability risk disclosure
requirements.description: All financial products must describe how sustainability risks are integrated. Baseline category (no sustainability claims).

requirements.title: Products promoting environmental or social characteristics
requirements.description: 'Light green' products that promote E or S characteristics. Must disclose how characteristics are met and proportion of aligned investments.

requirements.title: Products with sustainable investment objectives
requirements.description: 'Dark green' products with explicit sustainable investment objectives. Must demonstrate how the objective is achieved and DNSH.

requirements.title: PAI at product level
requirements.description: Article 8 and 9 products must disclose how PAIs are considered.

mandatory_14: Energy consumption intensity per high impact climate sector

productClassification_AUM_EU.note: As of Q2 2024; Article 9 declined from peak due to reclassifications

name: Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive
description: Directive (EU) 2024/1760 requiring large companies to identify, prevent, mitigate, and account for adverse human rights and environmental impacts in their operations and value chains.
applicableTo: EU companies >1,000 employees and >EUR 450M net worldwide turnover
Non-EU companies >EUR 450M net turnover generated in EU

requirements.title: Due diligence policy
requirements.description: Adopt and put into effect a due diligence policy covering human rights and environmental impacts.

requirements.title: Identifying actual and potential adverse impacts
requirements.description: Map operations and value chain to identify adverse impacts on human rights and environment.

requirements.title: Preventing and mitigating potential adverse impacts
requirements.description: Take appropriate measures to prevent, or where not possible, mitigate potential adverse impacts.

requirements.title: Bringing actual adverse impacts to an end
requirements.description: Bring actual adverse impacts to an end and provide remediation.

requirements.title: Climate transition plan
requirements.description: Adopt and implement a transition plan for climate change mitigation aligned with 1.5C / Paris Agreement.

requirements.title: Civil liability
requirements.description: Companies can be held civilly liable for damage caused by failure to comply with due diligence obligations.

penalties: Fines up to 5% of worldwide net turnover. Civil liability. Naming and shaming by supervisory authorities.

data.scopeNarrowedFrom_original: Original proposal had lower thresholds (500 employees); scope reduced in final text
data.valueChainScope: Direct and indirect business partners in upstream; direct partners in downstream
data.annex_humanRights: References ICCPR, ICESCR, UNCRC, ILO conventions
data.annex_environment: References Paris Agreement, CBD, Basel Convention, Minamata Convention, Stockholm Convention

name: Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism
description: Regulation (EU) 2023/956 imposing a carbon price on imports of carbon-intensive goods to prevent carbon leakage and ensure a level playing field with EU ETS.
applicableTo: Importers of covered goods into EU

requirements.title: CBAM reporting obligations (transitional)
requirements.description: Quarterly reports on embedded emissions in imported goods during transitional period (Oct 2023 - Dec 2025).

requirements.title: CBAM certificates
requirements.description: From 2026, importers must purchase and surrender CBAM certificates corresponding to embedded emissions, at EU ETS carbon price.

requirements.title: Calculation of embedded emissions
requirements.description: Must calculate actual embedded emissions based on production data; default values available as fallback.

penalties: Penalties at least 3x the average EU ETS certificate price for unreported emissions.

timeline.2023-2025: Transitional period - reporting only, no financial obligations
timeline.2026: Definitive system begins - certificates must be purchased
timeline.2026-2034: Gradual phase-in of CBAM as free EU ETS allowances phase out

calculationMethodology.embeddedEmissions: Direct emissions from production + indirect emissions from electricity (for some products)
calculationMethodology.deduction: Carbon price already paid in country of origin can be deducted
calculationMethodology.defaultValues: EU will provide default emission values based on worst-performing installations

data.keyImpact: Significant impact on exports from China, Russia, Turkey, India to EU in covered sectors

name: EU Emissions Trading System
description: Directive 2003/87/EC (as amended) establishing the world's first and largest carbon market. Cap-and-trade system covering ~40% of EU GHG emissions. Reformed under Fit for 55.

requirements.title: EU-wide cap and linear reduction factor
requirements.description: Overall emission cap decreasing by 4.3% per year from 2024 (increased from 2.2% under Fit for 55).

requirements.title: Surrender of allowances
requirements.description: Covered installations must surrender allowances equal to their verified emissions each year.

requirements.title: Monitoring, reporting and verification (MRV)
requirements.description: Annual emissions monitoring, reporting by operators, and third-party verification.

requirements.title: Free allocation and benchmarks
requirements.description: Free allowances allocated based on product benchmarks. Free allocation being phased out 2026-2034 as CBAM phases in.

penalties: EUR 100 per tonne excess emissions + obligation to surrender missing allowances.

carbonPriceHistory_EUR_tCO2.note: Price peaked at ~EUR 100 in Feb 2023; Market Stability Reserve has supported prices since 2019

ETS2.description: New ETS for buildings and road transport starting 2027
ETS2.socialClimateFund: EUR 65 billion fund to support vulnerable households during transition

name: EU Deforestation Regulation
description: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 prohibiting the placing on the EU market of commodities and products linked to deforestation or forest degradation after December 2020 cutoff date.
applicableTo: Operators and traders of covered commodities in EU market

requirements.title: Prohibition
requirements.description: Covered products must be deforestation-free, produced in accordance with relevant legislation of the country of production, and covered by a due diligence statement.

requirements.title: Due diligence
requirements.description: Operators must collect geolocation data, assess risk of non-compliance, and mitigate identified risks.

requirements.title: Due diligence statement
requirements.description: Submit due diligence statement to competent authority before placing products on market or exporting.

penalties: Fines proportionate to environmental damage; up to 4% of annual EU turnover. Confiscation of products. Exclusion from public procurement.

data.geolocationRequirement: Plot-level geolocation coordinates required for all production areas
data.countryBenchmarking: EU will classify countries as low, standard, or high risk; due diligence requirements vary accordingly
data.timeline_note: Implementation delayed from Dec 2024 to Dec 2025 for large operators, June 2026 for SMEs

name: EU Green Bond Standard
description: Regulation (EU) 2023/2631 establishing voluntary requirements for European Green Bonds (EuGB), requiring alignment with EU Taxonomy and external review.
applicableTo: Bond issuers wishing to use the 'European Green Bond' or 'EuGB' designation

requirements.title: Use of proceeds
requirements.description: At least 85% of proceeds must be allocated to Taxonomy-aligned activities. Up to 15% flexibility pocket for activities not yet covered by Taxonomy.

requirements.title: Allocation reporting
requirements.description: Annual allocation reports showing how proceeds are deployed, reviewed by external reviewer.

requirements.title: External review
requirements.description: Pre-issuance and post-issuance external review by registered and ESMA-supervised reviewers.

name: EU Green Claims Directive (proposed)
description: Proposed EU Directive on substantiation and communication of environmental claims and environmental labeling. Aims to combat greenwashing by requiring scientific substantiation of green claims.
applicableTo: Companies making voluntary environmental claims to EU consumers

requirements.title: Substantiation requirement
requirements.description: Environmental claims must be substantiated by a recognized scientific methodology, based on primary data where possible, covering the full life cycle.

requirements.title: Verification
requirements.description: Claims must be verified by an accredited third-party verifier before use.

requirements.title: Communication rules
requirements.description: Claims must specify whether they relate to the entire product or specific aspects; cannot use generic terms ('green', 'eco-friendly') without substantiation.

penalties: Member States to set penalties. Expected to include fines and product withdrawal.

data.status: Proposed March 2023. Political agreement reached. Final adoption pending.
keyRestrictions: Ban on claims based solely on carbon offsets
Ban on unsubstantiated aggregate environmental scores
Only EU/Member State-recognized environmental labels allowed

name: Saudi Exchange ESG Disclosure Guidelines
description: Saudi Exchange (Tadawul) ESG Disclosure Guidelines issued in 2021, defining 29 ESG KPIs for listed companies. Initially comply-or-explain, with phased mandatory implementation.
applicableTo: All companies listed on the Saudi Exchange (Tadawul)

requirements.title: Annual ESG disclosure
requirements.description: Listed companies must disclose ESG performance annually, initially on comply-or-explain basis.

requirements.title: 29 ESG Metrics
requirements.description: Companies must report against 29 defined KPIs across Environmental (9), Social (10), and Governance (10) dimensions.

requirements.title: Board responsibility
requirements.description: Board must oversee ESG strategy and disclosure.

phasedMandatory.2024: Mandatory ESG reporting for top-tier listed companies

data.alignment: Aligned with GRI, SASB, TCFD, and Saudi Vision 2030

name: Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange ESG Disclosure Guidance
description: ADX ESG Disclosure Guidance (2023 update) requiring listed companies to report 31 ESG metrics. Mandatory for all ADX-listed entities.
applicableTo: All companies listed on Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange (ADX)

requirements.title: Annual ESG report
requirements.description: All ADX-listed companies must publish an annual ESG report or integrate ESG disclosures into annual report.

requirements.title: 31 ESG Metrics
requirements.description: Companies must report 31 defined metrics (10 Environmental, 11 Social, 10 Governance).

requirements.title: Third-party assurance
requirements.description: Encouraged to obtain independent third-party assurance on ESG data.

data.complianceRate_2023: 80%+ of listed companies publishing ESG reports

name: Qatar Stock Exchange ESG Guidance
description: Qatar Stock Exchange ESG Reporting Guidance requiring listed companies to provide ESG disclosures aligned with international frameworks.
applicableTo: All companies listed on Qatar Stock Exchange

requirements.title: ESG reporting
requirements.description: Listed companies should publish an annual ESG/sustainability report using recognized international frameworks.

requirements.title: GRI alignment
requirements.description: QSE recommends alignment with GRI Standards as the primary reporting framework.

requirements.title: Key metrics
requirements.description: Report on material ESG topics relevant to the company's sector and operations.

data.status: Voluntary but strongly encouraged; QSE provides capacity building and training
data.QSE_sustainability_index: QSE launched a sustainability index tracking ESG leaders
data.nationalContext: Qatar National Vision 2030 drives ESG integration

name: UAE Climate Change Law
description: UAE Federal Decree-Law No. 11 of 2024 on Climate Change, establishing the legal framework for the UAE's climate commitments including net-zero by 2050 and NDC implementation.

requirements.title: National emissions inventory
requirements.description: Establishment of a comprehensive national GHG emissions inventory and monitoring system.

requirements.title: GHG reporting for large emitters
requirements.description: Mandatory GHG emissions reporting for facilities exceeding specified thresholds.

requirements.title: Climate adaptation planning
requirements.description: Government entities must develop climate adaptation plans.

requirements.title: Carbon credits framework
requirements.description: Establishment of a national carbon credits and offsets framework.

keyProvisions: Codifies UAE Net Zero by 2050 Strategic Initiative
Empowers Ministry of Climate Change and Environment to set sector emission targets
Establishes carbon credit registry and voluntary carbon market rules
Introduces climate change risk assessment requirements for key infrastructure
Supports green hydrogen and clean energy development

UAE_NDC_targets.2030: 31% reduction in GHG emissions vs BAU

data.context: First comprehensive climate legislation in the GCC region; enacted during UAE COP28 presidency

name: Saudi Green Initiative
description: Saudi Green Initiative launched in 2021 as the national framework for Saudi Arabia's environmental sustainability commitments, including climate, afforestation, and ecosystem restoration.

requirements.title: National emission reduction
requirements.description: Saudi Arabia committed to reducing emissions by 278 MtCO2e annually by 2030 (from projected BAU).

requirements.title: Renewable energy targets
requirements.description: Generate 50% of electricity from renewable sources by 2030.

requirements.title: Afforestation
requirements.description: Plant 10 billion trees across Saudi Arabia.

requirements.title: Protected areas
requirements.description: Increase protected areas to over 30% of total land area.

data.investment: Over $185 billion committed to green economy programs

circularCarbonEconomy.note: Saudi Arabia promotes CCS/CCUS and blue hydrogen as part of its transition strategy

name: SCA Sustainable Finance Framework
description: UAE Securities and Commodities Authority framework for sustainable finance and ESG integration in capital markets, applicable to all UAE-listed companies and licensed financial institutions.

requirements.title: ESG disclosure for listed companies
requirements.description: All listed companies must disclose ESG information in their annual governance reports.

requirements.title: Green bond/sukuk framework
requirements.description: Framework for issuance of green, social, sustainability and sustainability-linked bonds/sukuk.

requirements.title: ESG fund classification
requirements.description: Requirements for funds marketed as ESG or sustainable, including minimum investment criteria.

keyElements: ESG disclosure requirements for listed companies
Green/sustainable bond listing rules on ADX and DFM
ESG fund classification and anti-greenwashing rules
Climate risk management guidance for financial institutions

name: SEC v. DWS Group (Greenwashing Investigation)
description: SEC investigation and settlement with DWS Group (Deutsche Bank's asset management arm) over allegations of misrepresenting ESG integration practices in its investment processes.
penalties: USD 19 million settlement with SEC. Additional EUR 800,000 fine from BaFin (German regulator).

data.parties: SEC and BaFin vs. DWS Group (Deutsche Bank subsidiary)
data.allegation: DWS overstated the extent to which ESG factors were integrated into its investment decision-making process; ESG was not consistently or systematically applied as claimed in marketing materials
data.outcome: Settlement without admission of wrongdoing. DWS paid $19M to SEC. CEO resigned.
data.keyLesson: Asset managers must ensure ESG integration claims in marketing match actual investment processes. Whistleblower protections critical.
data.timeline: Whistleblower complaint 2021; SEC investigation 2022; settlement September 2023
data.triggeredBy: Former DWS Head of Sustainability Desiree Fixler filed whistleblower complaint

name: HSBC Greenwashing Advertising Ruling
description: UK Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) ruled that HSBC advertisements were misleading by highlighting green initiatives without adequate context about the bank's ongoing fossil fuel financing.
penalties: Ads banned. Reputational damage. No financial penalty from ASA (non-financial regulator).

data.allegation: Two HSBC ads (2022) promoted the bank's tree-planting initiatives and net-zero commitments without disclosing that HSBC continued to finance fossil fuel expansion worth billions
data.outcome: Ads ruled misleading and banned. ASA found they omitted material information about HSBC's ongoing fossil fuel financing.
data.keyLesson: Selective green messaging without disclosing overall environmental impact constitutes greenwashing. Financial institutions must provide balanced picture of sustainability activities vs. total business.
data.impactOnIndustry: Led to widespread review of financial sector advertising. FCA subsequently published anti-greenwashing rule (November 2024).

name: Santos Net-Zero Greenwashing Challenge
description: Australasian Centre for Corporate Responsibility (ACCR) filed Federal Court proceedings against Santos Ltd alleging its net-zero plan and 'clean energy' claims were misleading.

data.allegation: Santos' 2020 annual report and climate report claimed a credible pathway to net-zero by 2040, relying heavily on unproven carbon capture and storage (CCS) and nature-based offsets; 'clean energy' description of natural gas was misleading
data.outcome: Case ongoing as of 2024. Partial win for ACCR: court ruled the case can proceed on merits.
data.keyLesson: Corporate net-zero plans relying on unproven or speculative technology (CCS, offsets) face legal challenge. 'Clean energy' label for fossil gas is contested.
data.significance: First Australian court challenge to a company's net-zero plan. Test case for climate litigation globally.

name: Milieudefensie v. Royal Dutch Shell
description: Landmark climate litigation where The Hague District Court ordered Royal Dutch Shell to reduce its worldwide CO2 emissions by 45% by 2030 (vs 2019), including Scope 3 emissions.
penalties: Court order to reduce emissions. Shell appealed.

data.parties: Milieudefensie (Friends of the Earth Netherlands) et al. v. Royal Dutch Shell plc
data.jurisdiction: Netherlands (The Hague District Court; appeal pending at The Hague Court of Appeal)
data.allegation: Shell's emission reduction policy was insufficient and violated its duty of care under Dutch civil law (Book 6, Section 162 Dutch Civil Code) and human rights obligations
data.outcome: Court ordered Shell to reduce net CO2 emissions by 45% by end of 2030 vs 2019 levels across entire corporate group (Scope 1, 2, and 3). Shell appealed; appeal heard November 2024.
data.keyLesson: Corporations can be held liable for climate impacts through duty-of-care doctrine. Scope 3 emissions obligations imposed. First time a court ordered a private company to align with Paris Agreement.

reductionOrder.target: Net 45% reduction in CO2 by end of 2030 vs 2019
reductionOrder.scope: Scope 1, 2, and 3 (including end-use of sold products)
reductionOrder.legalBasis: Dutch Civil Code unwritten standard of care interpreted through IPCC/Paris Agreement

name: SEC v. Goldman Sachs (ESG Fund Misrepresentation)
description: SEC settled charges against Goldman Sachs Asset Management for failures in policies and procedures involving ESG investments across two funds (ESG-labeled mutual fund and SMA strategy).

data.parties: SEC v. Goldman Sachs Asset Management, L.P.
data.allegation: Goldman Sachs failed to follow its own policies for ESG integration between April 2017 and February 2020. Investments in an ESG fund were not consistently vetted through ESG questionnaires, and the firm lacked written policies for ESG research in the initial period.
data.outcome: Goldman Sachs settled for $4M without admitting or denying findings. Required to implement compliance improvements.
data.keyLesson: Even if ESG claims are not fraudulent, failure to follow documented ESG processes is an enforcement risk. Policies must match practice.

name: BaFin Investigation of Deutsche Bank / DWS Greenwashing
description: German financial regulator BaFin investigated DWS Group after whistleblower allegations that ESG-related claims in its 2020 annual report overstated ESG asset integration.
penalties: EUR 800,000 fine. Dawn raid on DWS offices by prosecutors.

data.parties: BaFin and Frankfurt Public Prosecutor vs. DWS Group
data.allegation: DWS 2020 annual report stated that over half ($900B) of assets under management were ESG-integrated, but ESG criteria were not consistently applied across the claimed scope
data.outcome: BaFin fined DWS EUR 800,000. Frankfurt prosecutors conducted dawn raid. DWS CEO Asoka Woehrmann resigned. Led to parallel SEC investigation and $19M US settlement.
data.keyLesson: Regulatory scrutiny is cross-border. Whistleblowers are empowered. ESG AUM claims must be verifiable.
data.crossBorderCoordination: BaFin and SEC coordinated investigations, establishing precedent for cross-border ESG enforcement

name: New York v. JBS (Greenwashing Lawsuit)
description: New York Attorney General filed lawsuit against JBS, the world's largest meat company, alleging its net-zero by 2040 pledge and related sustainability claims were misleading.

data.parties: New York Attorney General v. JBS S.A. / JBS USA
data.allegation: JBS's net-zero by 2040 pledge was misleading because: the company's emissions were actually increasing; the claim lacked a credible plan; sustainability marketing misled consumers buying JBS products
data.keyLesson: Net-zero pledges without credible reduction plans face legal risk. Consumer-facing sustainability claims in food sector under increased scrutiny. State attorneys general are active enforcers.
data.significance: First lawsuit by a US state AG against a major food company for climate-related greenwashing

name: ClientEarth v. Shell Board of Directors
description: ClientEarth, as a Shell shareholder, filed derivative action against Shell's board of directors alleging breach of directors' duties by failing to adequately manage climate risk.

data.parties: ClientEarth v. Shell plc Board of Directors
data.allegation: Shell directors breached duties under UK Companies Act 2006 (s.172, s.174) by failing to adopt and implement a climate strategy aligned with Paris Agreement, exposing the company to material climate risk
data.outcome: Dismissed by High Court in May 2023 on procedural grounds (permission to continue derivative claim denied). Court did not rule on merits. ClientEarth appealed; appeal dismissed July 2023.
data.keyLesson: While this specific claim failed procedurally, it established that climate-related directors' duties claims are justiciable in principle. Increased focus on personal liability of directors for climate risk management.
data.significance: First derivative claim against a company board specifically for climate risk management failures

name: TotalEnergies Climate Litigation
description: Multiple climate lawsuits against TotalEnergies in French courts, including duty of vigilance claims and misleading advertising complaints.
applicableTo: Major energy companies operating in EU

cases.name: Notre Affaire a Tous et al. v. TotalEnergies
cases.basis: French Duty of Vigilance Law (Loi de vigilance, 2017)
cases.allegation: TotalEnergies' vigilance plan fails to adequately address climate change risks and align with Paris Agreement
cases.status: Ongoing; procedural ruling in 2024 allowed case to proceed

cases.name: Greenpeace France v. TotalEnergies (advertising)
cases.allegation: TotalEnergies advertising presenting itself as a 'major player in the energy transition' was misleading given ongoing fossil fuel expansion

data.keyLesson: France's Duty of Vigilance Law creates unique liability for large companies on environmental and human rights issues. Multiple legal avenues available for climate claims.
data.frenchDutyOfVigilanceLaw: Law No. 2017-399 requires companies with 5,000+ employees in France (or 10,000+ worldwide) to establish, publish, and implement a vigilance plan

name: ASIC v. Mercer Superannuation (Greenwashing)
description: Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) action against Mercer Superannuation for greenwashing in its Sustainable Plus investment options.
penalties: AUD 11.3 million (approximately USD 7.3 million) penalty ordered by Federal Court.

data.allegation: Mercer's 'Sustainable Plus' investment options held investments in companies involved in fossil fuels, alcohol, and gambling, contrary to marketing representations that these options excluded such investments
data.outcome: Federal Court imposed AUD 11.3M penalty (November 2023). Mercer consented to declarations of contravention.
data.keyLesson: Exclusion-based ESG claims must be accurately implemented. Pension funds face heightened scrutiny. ASIC is actively pursuing greenwashing enforcement.
data.ASIC_greenwashing_campaign: Part of ASIC's broader greenwashing enforcement sweep; 35+ interventions since 2023

name: Global Reporting Initiative Standards
description: The GRI Standards are the world's most widely used sustainability reporting standards, enabling organizations to report their impacts on the economy, environment, and people.
applicableTo: Most widely used sustainability reporting framework globally

requirements.title: Foundation 2021
requirements.description: Sets out the purpose, system, and key concepts for using GRI Standards. Establishes reporting principles and requirements.

requirements.title: General Disclosures 2021
requirements.description: Report organizational details, governance, strategy, policies, practices, stakeholder engagement, and reporting process.

requirements.title: Material Topics 2021
requirements.description: Describe process to determine material topics, list of material topics, and management of each topic.

requirements.title: Economic Standards
requirements.description: Economic performance (201), market presence (202), indirect impacts (203), procurement (204), anti-corruption (205), anti-competitive behavior (206), tax (207).

requirements.title: Environmental Standards
requirements.description: Materials (301), energy (302), water (303), biodiversity (304), emissions (305), waste (306), supplier environmental assessment (308).

requirements.title: Social Standards
requirements.description: Employment (401), OHS (403), training (404), diversity (405), non-discrimination (406), human rights (407-412), local communities (413), supplier social assessment (414), customer health (416), privacy (418).

data.approach: Impact materiality (organization's impact on economy, environment, and people)
data.GRI_ESRS_interoperability: ESRS and GRI have published interoperability mapping; GRI reporters can largely satisfy ESRS requirements

name: IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Standards - S1 General Requirements
description: IFRS S1 General Requirements for Disclosure of Sustainability-related Financial Information. Sets out overall requirements for sustainability disclosures for capital markets, focusing on enterprise value.
applicableTo: Jurisdictions adopting ISSB standards (voluntary adoption by jurisdiction)
Adopted/referenced by UK, Japan, Singapore, Hong Kong, Australia, Brazil, Nigeria, others

requirements.title: Objective and scope
requirements.description: Disclose information about sustainability-related risks and opportunities that could reasonably be expected to affect entity's cash flows, access to finance, or cost of capital.

requirements.title: Governance
requirements.description: Disclose governance body oversight of sustainability risks/opportunities, management's role, and competence.

requirements.title: Strategy
requirements.description: Disclose sustainability risks/opportunities, their effects on business model and value chain, strategy resilience, and financial effects.

requirements.title: Risk management
requirements.description: Disclose processes to identify, assess, prioritize, and monitor sustainability risks/opportunities.

requirements.title: Metrics and targets
requirements.description: Disclose metrics and targets used to measure and manage sustainability risks/opportunities.

data.developedBy: International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), part of IFRS Foundation
data.effectiveFor: Annual reporting periods beginning on or after 1 January 2024
data.TCFD_alignment: Fully incorporates TCFD recommendations; TCFD disbanded and responsibilities transferred to ISSB
data.interoperabilityWithESRS: ISSB and EFRAG have published interoperability guidance showing high degree of compatibility

name: IFRS S2 Climate-related Disclosures
description: IFRS S2 Climate-related Disclosures requires entities to disclose information about climate-related risks and opportunities, including Scope 1, 2, and 3 GHG emissions and transition plans.
applicableTo: Same jurisdictional adoption as ISSB S1

requirements.title: Governance of climate risks and opportunities
requirements.description: Disclose board and management oversight of climate-related risks and opportunities.

requirements.title: Strategy including scenario analysis
requirements.description: Disclose climate risks/opportunities, effects on strategy, scenario analysis, and transition plans.

requirements.title: Risk management for climate
requirements.description: Disclose processes for identifying, assessing, managing climate risks and integration with overall risk management.

requirements.title: GHG emissions - Scope 1 and 2
requirements.description: Disclose absolute gross Scope 1 and 2 GHG emissions in metric tonnes CO2e using GHG Protocol.

requirements.title: GHG emissions - Scope 3
requirements.description: Disclose absolute gross Scope 3 GHG emissions in metric tonnes CO2e. One-year relief period for first-time adopters.

requirements.title: Climate resilience and scenario analysis
requirements.description: Assess and disclose climate resilience using scenario analysis aligned with latest international agreements.

data.GHG_methodology: GHG Protocol Corporate Standard (Scope 1 & 2) and Corporate Value Chain Standard (Scope 3)
data.industryGuidance: Accompanied by industry-based guidance derived from SASB Standards for 68 industries
data.scope3_relief: First annual reporting period: Scope 3 disclosure not required (one-year transition)
data.scenarioAnalysis: Required; must include climate-related scenario consistent with latest international agreement on climate change

name: Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures
description: TCFD framework of recommendations for climate-related financial disclosures, organized around four pillars: Governance, Strategy, Risk Management, and Metrics & Targets. Now subsumed into ISSB.
applicableTo: Mandatory in UK, NZ, Japan, Singapore, HK

requirements.title: Governance (a)
requirements.description: Board's oversight of climate-related risks and opportunities.

requirements.title: Governance (b)
requirements.description: Management's role in assessing and managing climate-related risks and opportunities.

requirements.title: Strategy (a)
requirements.description: Climate-related risks and opportunities identified over short, medium, and long term.

requirements.title: Strategy (b)
requirements.description: Impact of climate-related risks and opportunities on the organization's businesses, strategy, and financial planning.

requirements.title: Strategy (c)
requirements.description: Resilience of organization's strategy under different climate scenarios, including a 2C or lower scenario.

requirements.title: Risk Management (a)
requirements.description: Processes for identifying and assessing climate-related risks.

requirements.title: Risk Management (b)
requirements.description: Processes for managing climate-related risks.

requirements.title: Risk Management (c)
requirements.description: How climate risk processes are integrated into overall risk management.

requirements.title: Metrics & Targets (a)
requirements.description: Metrics used to assess climate-related risks and opportunities in line with strategy and risk management.

requirements.title: Metrics & Targets (b)
requirements.description: Scope 1, Scope 2, and if appropriate, Scope 3 GHG emissions and related risks.

requirements.title: Metrics & Targets (c)
requirements.description: Targets used to manage climate-related risks and opportunities, and performance against targets.

regulatoryMandates.UK: Mandatory for premium listed companies, large private companies, LLPs (2022)
regulatoryMandates.Japan: Mandatory for prime-listed companies on TSE (2023)
regulatoryMandates.HongKong: Mandatory for all listed companies by 2025

data.status: TCFD disbanded October 2023; responsibilities transferred to ISSB. ISSB S1/S2 fully incorporate and supersede TCFD recommendations.
data.legacy: TCFD framework is the foundation of all major climate disclosure standards (ISSB, ESRS, SEC proposal)

name: CDP (formerly Carbon Disclosure Project)
description: CDP operates the global environmental disclosure system for companies, cities, states, and regions. Over 23,000 companies disclose through CDP, representing $67 trillion in market capitalization.
applicableTo: 746 financial institutions with $136 trillion request disclosure

requirements.title: Climate Change Questionnaire
requirements.description: Comprehensive disclosure on governance, risks/opportunities, strategy, targets, emissions (Scope 1/2/3), energy, and verification.

requirements.title: Forests Questionnaire
requirements.description: Disclosure on commodity-driven deforestation risks across palm oil, soy, timber, cattle, cocoa, rubber, coffee.

requirements.title: Water Security Questionnaire
requirements.description: Disclosure on water risk assessment, water accounting, targets, governance, and value chain engagement.

criteria.C: Awareness - evidence of knowledge of environmental issues
criteria.B: Management - evidence of actions to address issues

scoringMethodology.A_list_2023: Over 400 companies scored A across all three programs

data.ISSB_alignment: CDP has aligned its questionnaire with ISSB S2 from 2024 disclosure cycle; acts as primary channel for ISSB-aligned disclosure

name: Science Based Targets Initiative - Target Setting Requirements
description: SBTi provides a framework for companies to set GHG emission reduction targets aligned with climate science. Targets must be validated by SBTi and are based on sector-specific decarbonization pathways.
applicableTo: Over 9,000 companies committed; 4,200+ with validated targets

requirements.title: Near-term targets (required)
requirements.description: Companies must set near-term targets (5-10 years) to reduce Scope 1 and 2 emissions. 1.5C-aligned minimum 4.2% annual linear reduction.

requirements.title: Scope 3 target requirement
requirements.description: If Scope 3 emissions are more than 40% of total Scope 1+2+3, a Scope 3 target covering at least 67% of Scope 3 is required.

requirements.title: Net-zero targets (optional but encouraged)
requirements.description: Long-term targets to reach net-zero by 2050 at latest. Must reduce at least 90% of Scope 1+2+3; neutralize residual <10% with permanent CDR.

requirements.title: FLAG targets
requirements.description: Companies in land-intensive sectors (with >20% FLAG emissions) must set FLAG targets: zero deforestation by 2025, emissions pathway aligned with 1.5C.

requirements.title: Validation and annual reporting
requirements.description: Targets must be submitted to SBTi for validation. Companies must report progress annually via CDP.

targetSettingMethods.absoluteContraction: Reduce total emissions by required percentage (4.2%/yr for 1.5C)
targetSettingMethods.sectoralDecarbonization: Reduce emissions intensity following sector-specific pathways

netZero_criteria.residualNeutralization: Remaining <10% must be neutralized with permanent CDR
netZero_criteria.beyondValueChain_mitigation: Companies are expected to invest in BVCM in addition to in-value-chain reductions

financialInstitutions.standard: SBTi Financial Institutions Net-Zero Standard (launched October 2024)
financialInstitutions.requirement: Portfolio alignment targets using sectoral pathways and portfolio coverage approach

data.controversy_2024: SBTi Board decision in April 2024 to potentially allow carbon credits for Scope 3 targets was controversial and faced significant pushback from staff and stakeholders; subsequently paused for consultation

name: Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures
description: TNFD provides a risk management and disclosure framework for organizations to report and act on evolving nature-related dependencies, impacts, risks, and opportunities.

requirements.title: Governance
requirements.description: Disclose governance around nature-related dependencies, impacts, risks, and opportunities.

requirements.title: Strategy
requirements.description: Disclose actual and potential effects of nature-related issues on strategy and financial planning.

requirements.title: Risk & Impact Management
requirements.description: Disclose processes for identifying, assessing, prioritizing, and managing nature-related dependencies, impacts, risks, and opportunities.

requirements.title: Metrics & Targets
requirements.description: Disclose metrics and targets used to assess and manage nature-related issues. Includes core global and core sector metrics.

LEAPapproach.L: Locate interface with nature (priority locations, biomes, ecosystems)
LEAPapproach.E: Evaluate dependencies and impacts on nature

coreGlobalMetrics: Pollutant releases to soil, water, air
Water withdrawal and consumption in water-stressed areas

alignment.ISSB: TNFD designed to work with ISSB S1/S2
alignment.GBF: Aligned with Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (Target 15)
alignment.ESRS_E4: Compatible with ESRS E4 Biodiversity standard

data.earlyAdopters: 320+ organizations from 46 countries including Nestle, GSK, UBS, Norges Bank Investment Management

name: SASB Industry Standards (now part of ISSB)
description: SASB Standards identify financially material sustainability topics and metrics for 77 industries across 11 sectors. Now maintained by the ISSB as industry-based guidance accompanying IFRS S1/S2.
applicableTo: Companies in 77 industries (voluntary; referenced by investors and ISSB)

requirements.title: Industry-specific disclosure topics
requirements.description: Each industry standard identifies 3-8 disclosure topics deemed financially material for that industry.

requirements.title: Quantitative and qualitative metrics
requirements.description: Specific metrics per topic, including activity metrics (denominators) and accounting metrics.

data.approach: Financial materiality (investor-focused); identifies what's material per industry

topics: Security, Human Rights & Rights of Indigenous Peoples
Incorporation of ESG Factors in Credit Analysis

data.status: SASB Standards consolidated under IFRS Foundation / ISSB in 2022. Continue as the industry-based guidance layer for ISSB S1/S2 disclosures.

name: ISO 14001 Environmental Management System
description: ISO 14001:2015 specifies requirements for an environmental management system (EMS). Widely adopted; over 500,000 certifications globally. Often a prerequisite for ESG-credible operations.
applicableTo: Any organization seeking to manage environmental responsibilities systematically

requirements.title: Environmental policy
requirements.description: Top management must establish an environmental policy appropriate to the organization's context.

requirements.title: Environmental aspects and impacts
requirements.description: Identify environmental aspects of activities, products, and services and determine significant impacts.

requirements.title: Compliance obligations
requirements.description: Identify and access applicable legal and other requirements.

requirements.title: Objectives and planning
requirements.description: Set measurable environmental objectives and plans to achieve them.

requirements.title: Operational control
requirements.description: Implement controls to manage significant environmental aspects.

requirements.title: Performance evaluation
requirements.description: Monitor, measure, analyze, and evaluate environmental performance.

requirements.title: Management review and continual improvement
requirements.description: Top management must periodically review the EMS and drive continual improvement.

data.annex_SL_structure: High-Level Structure (Annex SL) compatible with ISO 9001, ISO 45001, ISO 50001
data.notAPerformanceStandard: ISO 14001 certifies the management system, not environmental performance levels

name: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights
description: The UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (2011) establish the authoritative global standard on business and human rights, structured around three pillars: Protect, Respect, Remedy.
applicableTo: Referenced by CSDDD, ESRS, OECD Guidelines, SBTi minimum safeguards, EU Taxonomy

requirements.title: Pillar I: State Duty to Protect
requirements.description: States must protect against human rights abuse by third parties, including business enterprises.

requirements.title: Pillar II: Corporate Responsibility to Respect
requirements.description: Business enterprises should respect human rights through due diligence and remediation.

requirements.title: Pillar III: Access to Remedy
requirements.description: Effective judicial and non-judicial grievance mechanisms must be available to victims.

requirements.title: Human rights due diligence
requirements.description: Companies must conduct human rights due diligence to identify, prevent, mitigate, and account for adverse human rights impacts.

requirements.title: Ongoing process
requirements.description: Due diligence must be ongoing, covering the enterprise's own activities, value chain, and business relationships.

threePillars: State Duty to Protect human rights
Corporate Responsibility to Respect human rights
Access to Remedy for victims of business-related abuse
dueDiligenceElements: Identify and assess actual and potential adverse impacts
Integrate findings and take appropriate action
data.legalSignificance: Soft law but increasingly hardened through: EU CSDDD, French Duty of Vigilance Law, German Supply Chain Act, EU Taxonomy minimum safeguards
data.salienceApproach: UNGPs use 'salience' not 'materiality' - most salient human rights risks based on severity and likelihood

name: ICMA Green Bond Principles
description: International Capital Market Association (ICMA) Green Bond Principles provide voluntary process guidelines for issuing green bonds, covering use of proceeds, evaluation, management of proceeds, and reporting.
applicableTo: Market standard for green bond issuance globally

requirements.title: Use of proceeds
requirements.description: Proceeds must be used for eligible green projects (renewable energy, energy efficiency, clean transport, etc.).

requirements.title: Process for project evaluation and selection
requirements.description: Issuer must clearly communicate environmental sustainability objectives and process for determining eligible projects.

requirements.title: Management of proceeds
requirements.description: Net proceeds must be tracked and managed in a sub-account or portfolio, verified by issuer.

requirements.title: Reporting
requirements.description: Annual report on allocation of proceeds and, where possible, environmental impact.

fourComponents: Process for Project Evaluation and Selection
eligibleCategories: Environmentally sustainable management of natural resources and land use
data.externalReview: Recommended but not mandatory (second party opinion, verification, certification, or scoring/rating)
data.marketSize_2024: Cumulative green bond issuance exceeded $2.5 trillion globally

name: OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises on Responsible Business Conduct
description: OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises (updated 2023) provide recommendations on responsible business conduct covering human rights, environment, anti-corruption, consumer interests, and more.
applicableTo: Multinational enterprises operating in or from OECD member countries
Referenced by EU Taxonomy, CSDDD, ESRS as minimum safeguards

requirements.title: Human rights due diligence
requirements.description: Carry out risk-based due diligence to identify, prevent, mitigate adverse impacts on human rights, labour, environment.

requirements.title: Environment chapter
requirements.description: Establish environmental management systems; assess and manage environmental impacts throughout value chain; take precautionary approach.

requirements.title: Climate change (2023 update)
requirements.description: New provisions on climate change including setting science-based emission reduction targets and transition planning.

requirements.title: National Contact Points (NCPs)
requirements.description: Governments must establish NCPs to handle complaints (specific instances) against companies for alleged non-observance.

2023_update_highlights: Strengthened environmental and climate due diligence expectations
New recommendations on climate change mitigation and adaptation
Enhanced technology and digital responsibility chapter
Expanded due diligence to cover all adverse impacts (not just human rights)
data.NCP_mechanism: Quasi-judicial grievance mechanism; NCPs mediate disputes between complainants and companies
data.relationship_to_EU_law: EU Taxonomy minimum safeguards reference OECD Guidelines; CSDDD aligned with OECD due diligence standards

name: Net Zero Asset Owner Alliance Protocol
description: UN-convened Net Zero Asset Owner Alliance target-setting protocol for asset owners committing to net-zero portfolio emissions by 2050, with interim targets.
applicableTo: Asset owners (pension funds, insurers, sovereign wealth funds) committed to NZAOA

requirements.title: Portfolio decarbonization targets
requirements.description: Set interim targets to reduce portfolio financed emissions: 22-32% by 2025, 40-60% by 2030 vs 2019 baseline.

requirements.title: Sector targets
requirements.description: Set targets for high-emitting sectors in portfolio (utilities, oil & gas, steel, transport, real estate).

requirements.title: Engagement targets
requirements.description: Engage with investee companies to set science-based targets and align with net-zero.

requirements.title: Financing transition targets
requirements.description: Increase allocation to climate solutions (renewable energy, green bonds, climate tech).

name: Transition Plan Taskforce Disclosure Framework
description: UK Transition Plan Taskforce (TPT) Disclosure Framework setting out good practice for transition plan disclosures, designed to complement ISSB and be globally applicable.
applicableTo: All entities disclosing transition plans (voluntary; expected to become UK requirement)

requirements.title: Ambition
requirements.description: Disclose objectives, priorities, and interim/long-term targets with clear timeframes.

requirements.title: Action
requirements.description: Disclose implementation strategy including decarbonization levers, business and operational changes, and engagement with value chain.

requirements.title: Accountability
requirements.description: Disclose governance, roles and responsibilities, skills, incentives, and progress tracking.

fiveElements: Engagement Strategy (value chain, industry, government, communities)
Metrics and Targets (GHG, energy, financial allocation)
data.designedToAccompany: ISSB S2 transition plan disclosures; ESRS E1 transition plan requirements
data.UK_adoption: Expected to be incorporated into UK Sustainability Disclosure Standards (UK SDS)
data.sectorGuidance: Sector-specific guidance published for 7 sectors (asset management, banking, electric utilities, food & beverage, metals & mining, oil & gas, asset owners)
